content.full_text:
STATE OF CALIFORNIA
FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION
1102 Q Street • Suite 3000 • Sacramento, CA 95811
(916) 322-5660 • Fax (916) 322-0886

November 19, 2018

Alex Myers
Myers & Associates
1219 Coombs Street, Suite 200
Napa, CA 94559

Re: Your Request for Informal Assistance
Our File No. I-18-242

Dear Mr. Myers:

This letter responds to your request for advice regarding the conflict of interest provisions of the Political Reform Act (the "Act").¹ Please note that we are only providing advice under the conflict of interest provisions of the Act and not under other general conflict of interest prohibitions such as common law conflict of interest or Section 1090. Because your inquiry general in nature and does not pertain to a specific governmental decision, we are treating it as one for informal assistance.²

QUESTION

Do the Act's conflict of interest provisions prohibit you from taking part in Planning Commission decisions involving development applications where a client of your law firm provides free consulting services to the applicant in the hope of ultimately being selected to list and sell the homes?

CONCLUSION

Yes. For the reasons provided below, the Act's conflict of interest provisions would prohibit you from taking part in Planning Commission decisions involving these development applications because it is reasonably foreseeable that your client will receive a measurable financial benefit from this type of decision.

________________

¹ The Political Reform Act is contained in Government Code Sections 81000 through 91014. All statutory references are to the Government Code, unless otherwise indicated. The regulations of the Fair Political Practices Commission are contained in Sections 18110 through 18997 of Title 2 of the California Code of Regulations. All regulatory references are to Title 2, Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations, unless otherwise indicated.

² Informal assistance does not provide the requestor with the immunity provided by an opinion or formal written advice. (Section 83114; Regulation 18329(c)(3).)

File No. I-18-242
Page No. 2

FACTS AS PRESENTED BY REQUESTER

You are a Planning Commissioner for the City of Napa. You have a law practice there and
one of your clients is a property management company (the "PMC"). You regularly perform work
for the PMC in the form of landlord-tenant law advice and representation, and have received in
excess of $500.00 in the last 12 months for your work. The PMC is a corporation that is wholly-
owned by a husband and wife. The husband is a real estate broker who provides consulting advice
for free to property developers on infill housing projects in the hope that developers will then
choose him to list and sell the developed homes.

When developers become applicants and their projects come before the Planning
Commission, the owner of the PMC is not the applicant, not in contract with the applicant, and
not being paid by the applicant; however, he may address the Planning Commission as a consultant for
a project during the applicant's presentation. After a project is built and completed, he is often then
selected to be the broker to list and sell the homes. You have always recused yourself whenever
your client has been involved in these development projects.

ANALYSIS

The Act's conflict of interest provisions prohibit a public official from making, participating
in making, or using his or her official position to influence a governmental decision that would have
a reasonably foreseeable material financial effect on one or more of the official's financial interests
distinguishable from the decision's effect on the public generally. (Sections 87100 and 87103.)

An official's financial interests that may give rise to a disqualifying conflict of interest
under the Act are set forth in Section 87103 and include all of the following:

• Any business entity in which the official has a direct or indirect investment worth $2,000 or
more (subdivision (a)), or in which the official is a director, officer, partner, trustee,
employee, or holds any position of management (subdivision (d)).

• Any real property in which the official has a direct or indirect interest worth $2,000 or more.
(Subdivision (b).)

• Any source of income aggregating $500 or more in value to the official in the 12 months
prior to when the decision at issue is made. (Subdivision (c).)

• Any donor of a gift or gifts aggregating $470 or more to the official in the 12 months prior
to when the decision at issue is made. (Subdivision (e); Regulation 18940.2.)

• An interest in the official's personal finances, including those of the official's immediate
family. This is known as the "personal financial effects" rule. An official always has an
interest in his or her personal finances.

Based on the facts provided, you would have an interest in your law practice as a business
entity and source of income. You also have an interest in any client of your law practice, including

File No. I-18-242
Page No. 3

the PMC, from whom you have received income aggregating $500 or more in the previous 12
months as a source of income. Moreover, you have a source of income interest in the PMC owners
(husband and wife) due to their sole ownership of the PMC. Regulation 18700.1(a)(2)(A), defining
"source of income," states:

In addition to having a financial interest in any business entity from which the official has
received income of $500 or more within 12 months before the relevant governmental
decision is made, the official has a source-of-income financial interest in all of the
following:

(A) Any individual owning a 50 percent or greater interest in that business entity.

Accordingly, you may not make, participate in making or use your official position to
influence any government decision if it is reasonably foreseeable that the government decision will
have a material financial effect upon your law practice, the PMC, or the owners of the PMC.

Foreseeability and Materiality

Standards for foreseeability vary depending on whether an interest is explicitly involved in a
governmental decision. An official's financial interest is explicitly involved in a decision, and is
presumed to be reasonably foreseeable, if the interest is a named party in, or the subject of, a
decision before the official or the official's agency. (Regulation 18701(a)).³ For a financial interest
that is not explicitly involved in the decision at issue, the financial effect of the decision on an
official's interest is reasonably foreseeable if it can be recognized as a realistic possibility and more
than hypothetical or theoretical. (Regulation 18701(b)).⁴

Source-of-Income: Owner of the PMC Who Consults for Developers.

Regulation 18702.3(a)(2) provides a decision's effect on an official's source-of-income
interest in an individual is material if the individual will receive a measurable financial benefit or
loss⁵ from the decision (Regulation 18702.5), or if the official knows or has reason to know that the
individual has a business interest or real-property interest that will be materially affected by the
decision at issue under the materiality standards applicable to that interest (Regulation 18702.1 and
18702.2, respectively).

___________________________

³ A financial interest is the subject of a proceeding if the decision involves the issuance, renewal, approval,
denial, or revocation of any license, permit, or other entitlement to, or contract with, the financial interest, and includes
any governmental decision affecting a real property financial interest as described in Regulation 18702.2(a)(1)-(6).
(Regulation 18701(a).)

⁴ Under Commission regulations, you are disqualified from any decision in which the PMC is explicitly
involved and you have stated that PMC may appear before the Planning Commission as a consultant. However, even if
appearing as a consultant, it is unnecessary to determine whether the PMC is explicitly involved in any particular
decision in light of our conclusion below that you are disqualified from the decisions even under the more lenient
materiality standards for interests not explicitly involved in the decision.

⁵ Note in January 2015, the Commission removed the $250 and replaced it with the current standard of
"measurable financial benefit or loss" which is determined on a case-by-case basis.

File No. 1-18-242
Page No. 4

Based on the facts provided, the owner of the PMC provides free consulting services to applicant property developers, which may include addressing the Planning Commission during the applicant's presentation, in the hope that he will ultimately be selected as the broker to list and sell the homes. When the Planning Commission approves an application where the PMC owner has provided free consulting services to the developer, it is reasonable to expect that the PMC owner's expectations and chances of being selected as the broker increase as a direct result. Certainly, there would be no other reason to provide free upfront consulting services. Under these circumstances, it is therefore reasonably foreseeable that the PMC owner will receive a measurable financial benefit from this type of decision.

Accordingly, you may not make, participate in making or use your official position to influence any government decision on a development application where the PMC owner provides free consulting services to the applicant.

If you have other questions on this matter, please contact me at (916) 322-5660.

Sincerely,

Dave Bainbridge
General Counsel

By: Jack Woodside
Senior Counsel, Legal Division

JW:jgl

sections.question:
Do the Act's conflict of interest provisions prohibit you from taking part in Planning 
Commission decisions involving development applications where a client of your law firm provides 
free consulting services to the applicant in the hope of ultimately being selected to list and sell the 
homes?
sections.conclusion:
Yes. For the reasons provided below, the Act's conflict of interest provisions would prohibit 
you from taking part in Planning Commission decisions involving these development applications 
because it is reasonably foreseeable that your client will receive a measurable financial benefit from 
this type of decision.2 Informal assistance does not provide the requestor with the immunity provided by an opinion or formal 
written advice. (Section 83114; Regulation 18329(c)(3).)

in making, or using his or her official position to influence a governmental decision that would have

distinguishable from the decision's effect on the public generally. (Sections 87100 and 87103.) 
An official's financial interests that may give rise to a disqualifying conflict of interest 
under the Act are set forth in Section 87103 and include all of the following: 
• 
Any business entity in which the official has a direct or indirect investment worth $2,000 or 
more (subdivision (a)), or in which the official is a director, officer, partner, trustee, 
employee, or holds any position of management (subdivision (d)). 
• 
Any real property in which the official has a direct or indirect interest worth $2,000 or more. 
(Subdivision (b).) 
• 
Any source of income aggregating $500 or more in value to the official in the 12 months 
prior to when the decision at issue is made. (Subdivision (c).) 
• 
Any donor of a gift or gifts aggregating $4 70 or more to the official in the 12 months prior 
to when the decision at issue is made. (Subdivision (e); Regulation 18940.2.) 
• 
An interest in the official's personal finances, including those of the official's immediate 
family. This is known as the "personal financial effects" rule. An official always has an 
interest in his or her personal finances. 
Based on the facts provided, you would have an interest in your law practice as a business

(husband and wife) due to their sole ownership of the PMC. Regulation 18700.l(a)(2)(A), defining 
'.'source of income," states: 
In addition to having a financial interest in any business entity from which the official has 
received income of $500 or more within 12 months before the relevant governmental

following: 
(A) Any individual owning a 50 percent or greater interest in that business entity. 
Accordingly, you may not make, participate in making or use your official position to 
influence any government decision if it is reasonably foreseeable that the government decision will

that is not explicitly involved in the decision at issue, the financial effect of the decision on an

Regulation 18702.3(a)(2) provides a decision's effect on an official's source-of-income 
interest in an individual is material if the individual will receive a measurable financial benefit or 
loss5 from the decision (Regulation 18702.5), or if the official knows or has reason to know that the 
individual has a business interest or real-property interest that will be materially affected by the

18702.2, respectively). 
3 A financial interest is the subject ofa proceeding if the decision involves the issuance, renewal, approval, 
denial, or revocation of any license, permit, or other entitlement to, or contract with, the financial interest, and includes 
any governmental decision affecting a real property financial interest as described in Regulation 18702.2(a)(l)-(6). 
(Regulation 1870l(a).) 
4 Under Commission regulations, you are disqualified from any decision in which the PMC is explicitly 
involved and you have stated that PMC may appear before the Planning Commission as a consultant. However, even if 
appearing as a consultant, it is unnecessary to determine whether the PMC is explicitly involved in any particular 
decision in light of our conclusion below that you are disqualified from the decisions even under the more lenient 
materiality standards for interests not explicitly involved in the decision. 
5 Note in January 2015, the Commission removed the $250 and replaced it with the current standard of 
"measurable financial benefit or loss" which is determined on a case-by-case basis.  
Based on the facts provided, the owner of the PMC provides free consulting services to 
applicant property developers, which may include addressing the Planning Commission during the 
applicant's presentation, in the hope that he will ultimately be selected as the broker to list and sell 
the homes. When the Planning Commission approves an application where the PMC owner has 
provided free consulting services to the developer, it is reasonable to expect the PMC owner's 
expectations and chances of being selected as the broker increase as a direct result. Certainly, there 
would be no other reason to provide free upfront consulting services. Under these circumstances, it

Accordingly, you may not make, participate in making or use your official position to 
influence any government decision on a development application where the PMC owner provides
